rationale_for_baseline_tier: An unheralded syncope at rest preceded by palpitations, in a young adult with recurrent palpitations and a family history of sudden cardiac arrest at 28 and early-onset atrial fibrillation, is a major red flag for a lethal inherited arrhythmia (short QT syndrome or another channelopathy). The mechanism of collapse is presumed malignant ventricular arrhythmia, which can recur without warning and cause sudden death within minutes. Although the patient is currently haemodynamically recovered with normal vitals, transfer must be by emergency ambulance with continuous cardiac monitoring and immediate defibrillation capability — not self-transport — because arrest could occur en route. This is T5 (call 000). The defining test, a 12-lead ECG QTc, is only obtainable in hospital, so the telehealth stance is emergency escalation, not remote reassurance or diagnosis.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Recognised high-risk syncope (unheralded, at rest, arrhythmic prodrome, family history of young sudden death) at first assessment
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — call 000 immediately for a monitored emergency ambulance
escalation_edges.rationale: The constellation identifies a potentially lethal arrhythmic cause of syncope requiring immediate continuous monitoring and defibrillation capability during transfer.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because you fainted suddenly with no warning, felt your heart race just before, and your brother had a cardiac arrest young, I'm very concerned this is a heart-rhythm problem. I need you to call 000 for an ambulance right now — you must be monitored on the way to hospital, not drive yourself.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Recurrence of palpitations, presyncope or a further faint before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.rationale: Recurrent arrhythmic symptoms herald a further malignant arrhythmia; the patient must stay flat and monitored and 000 must be updated.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your heart races again, you feel faint, or you pass out again before the ambulance gets there, stay lying down and tell 000 straight away — do not get up.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse with unresponsiveness / no normal breathing (cardiac arrest)
escalation_edges.rationale: Sudden cardiac arrest from ventricular fibrillation is the feared outcome; bystander CPR and early defibrillation are the only life-saving interventions and the sibling must act at once.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he collapses and won't wake up or isn't breathing normally, tell 000 immediately, lay him flat on his back, and start pushing hard and fast in the centre of his chest — keep going until the paramedics take over. If there's a defibrillator nearby, send someone for it.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young adult who does not feel safe alone, with a sibling currently present
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: The sibling must stay in the room at all times until paramedics arrive, keep the patient lying flat, and be ready to call 000 back and start CPR if the patient collapses. Ensure the front door is unlocked for paramedics.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Otherwise-well 24-year-old with normal current vitals and a structurally normal heart
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Apparent wellness must NOT down-tier the response — the arrhythmic substrate is intermittent and the family history makes sudden death a real risk; maintain full emergency escalation.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and say there has been a sudden faint with a racing heartbeat and a family history of young sudden cardiac death. Lie flat and stay completely still until the paramedics arrive — do not stand, walk, or drive. Your sibling should stay with you the whole time. If you faint again or your heart races, stay down and tell 000. If you collapse and can't be woken, your sibling should start CPR immediately.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for GP or clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the hospital with the full history: unheralded syncope at rest, preceding palpitations, six months of recurrent palpitations, brother's sudden cardiac arrest at 28 and mother's early atrial fibrillation. The hospital discharge summary and any ECG/monitoring results should go to the GP, with a plan for electrophysiology follow-up and family screening.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the event as a benign faint and advising self-care or routine/prompt GP review, missing the high-risk arrhythmic syncope.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient suffers a recurrent polymorphic VT/VF at home or unmonitored and dies of a preventable sudden cardiac arrest.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising urgent same-day in-person or urgent-care review but without emergency ambulance and continuous monitoring, allowing unmonitored travel.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is the correct tier here, so over-triage is not a meaningful risk in this case; the dominant, heavily weighted danger is under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The definitive diagnosis cannot be made or excluded via telehealth because a 12-lead ECG QTc is unavailable, and the patient currently looks and feels well with normal vitals.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate on the high-risk clinical pattern regardless of current wellness — treat as a potentially lethal arrhythmic syncope until hospital monitoring and ECG say otherwise.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the normal current state, the structurally normal heart, or the youth of the patient to reassure and down-tier to routine follow-up.
